skill.name: Azure Identity SDK for Python
skill.description: Azure Identity SDK for Python authentication. Use for DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, service principals, and token caching.
skill.summary: Master Azure authentication with DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, and service principal patterns for Python applications.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis scanned 0 files with 0 total lines. No executable code or suspicious patterns detected. This is a documentation skill providing guidance on Azure Identity SDK usage. Risk score is 0/100. Safe to publish.

content.value_statement: Developers struggle with Azure SDK authentication across local development and cloud deployments. This skill provides unified credential patterns that work seamlessly in both environments.
actual_capabilities: Configure DefaultAzureCredential for local development and Azure deployment
Implement managed identity authentication for Azure-hosted applications
Set up service principal authentication with client secrets or certificates
Retrieve access tokens for specific Azure resource scopes
Implement async credential handling with proper resource cleanup
limitations: Requires Azure SDK for Python packages to be installed separately
Does not provide actual credential tokens or authentication keys
Requires proper Azure environment configuration for production use
Cannot bypass Azure security requirements or permission models

use_cases.title: Python Developer Building Azure Cloud Applications
use_cases.description: Implement seamless authentication that works locally with Azure CLI and in production with managed identity.
use_cases.target_user: Backend Python developers deploying to Azure App Service or Functions

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Setting Up CI/CD Pipelines
use_cases.description: Configure service principal authentication for automated deployments using environment variables.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers managing Azure DevOps or GitHub Actions workflows

use_cases.title: Solutions Architect Designing Multi-Environment Applications
use_cases.description: Create credential chains that handle development, staging, and production environments with appropriate auth methods.
use_cases.target_user: Cloud architects designing enterprise Azure solutions

prompt_templates.title: Basic Azure Authentication Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me authenticate my Python application with Azure using DefaultAzureCredential. I need to connect to Azure Blob Storage.

prompt_templates.title: Managed Identity Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use ManagedIdentityCredential for my Azure Function with a user-assigned managed identity. Include the required client_id parameter.

prompt_templates.title: Service Principal for CI/CD
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to set up ClientSecretCredential for my GitHub Actions pipeline. Show me the environment variables and Python code to authenticate with Azure.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Credential Chain with Token Retrieval
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a ChainedTokenCredential that tries managed identity first, then falls back to Azure CLI. Include code to get tokens for Azure Database for PostgreSQL.

output_examples.input: Basic DefaultAzureCredential setup for Blob Storage
output_examples.output: Configure DefaultAzureCredential, which automatically tries multiple authentication methods. Locally it uses Azure CLI credentials, while in Azure it uses managed identity.
Use the azure.identity.aio module with context managers. The async credential is automatically closed when exiting the async with block, ensuring proper resource cleanup.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential for code that runs both locally and in Azure without modification
Prefer managed identity over service principals for production Azure deployments
Exclude unused credential types to reduce authentication latency
anti_patterns: Hardcoding client secrets or connection strings directly in source code
Using InteractiveBrowserCredential in production server applications
Not closing async credentials explicitly, leading to resource leaks

faq.question: What is the recommended credential for most scenarios?
faq.answer: DefaultAzureCredential is recommended as it automatically selects the best authentication method based on the environment, working seamlessly in both local development and Azure deployments.

faq.question: How does DefaultAzureCredential choose which credential to use?
faq.answer: It tries credentials in order: EnvironmentCredential, WorkloadIdentityCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential, SharedTokenCacheCredential, VisualStudioCodeCredential, AzureCliCredential, AzurePowerShellCredential, and AzureDeveloperCliCredential.

faq.question: When should I use managed identity instead of service principal?
faq.answer: Use managed identity for applications running on Azure (VMs, App Service, Functions) as it eliminates credential management. Use service principals for CI/CD pipelines and non-Azure environments.

faq.question: How do I use a specific credential instead of the default chain?
faq.answer: Instantiate the specific credential class directly, such as ClientSecretCredential or AzureCliCredential, instead of DefaultAzureCredential.

faq.question: Can I customize the order of credentials in the chain?
faq.answer: Yes, use ChainedTokenCredential to create a custom chain with credentials in your preferred order of precedence.

faq.question: How do I handle async applications with Azure credentials?
faq.answer: Use the azure.identity.aio module and close credentials explicitly with await credential.close() or use async context managers for automatic cleanup.
